intro: December 1975. You run Kodak. You own 90% of American film sales and 85% of cameras. A 24-year-old engineer named Steve Sasson just walked into your office holding a toaster-sized prototype that takes pictures with no film at all.

realOutcome.summary: Kodak invented the digital camera, patented much of digital photography, and still went bankrupt fighting the future it built. Every decision below is real.

decisions.scenario: Sasson demos the filmless camera. It's ugly, slow, and 0.01 megapixels — and it makes your entire film business optional. He asks for funding to develop it.

choices.text: Fund a skunkworks. Cannibalize yourself before someone else does.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Kodak owns digital photography a decade before anyone else takes it seriously. The company that killed film is the company that sold it.

choices.text: Patent it, shelve it. 'That's cute — don't tell anyone.'
choices.reveal: This is what happened — Sasson later said management's response was literally 'that's cute, but don't tell anyone about it.' Kodak patented the tech and buried the product for fear of hurting film.

choices.text: Sell the patent. Filmless cameras aren't our business.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You just handed a rival the sword. At least in the real timeline the patents earned Kodak billions in licensing before the end — this way you don't even get that.

decisions.scenario: 1981. Sony announces the Mavica, a filmless electronic camera. Your own internal study concludes digital will replace film — but estimates you have about ten years before it's a real threat.

choices.text: Use the decade: shift R&D hard into digital now.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Ten years of head start with your patents and your budget means Kodak, not Sony or Canon, defines the digital camera. The report was right — you just actually used it.

choices.text: File the report. Ten years is forever. Film is the cash cow.
choices.reveal: This is what happened. The 1981 study accurately predicted the transition, and Kodak used its decade of warning to sell more film. The report aged perfectly; the strategy did not.

choices.text: Acquire Sony's camera division to control the threat.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Sony laughs you out of Tokyo, keeps the Mavica, and now knows exactly how scared you are. You can't buy every company that will eventually make a camera phone.

decisions.scenario: 1994. You quietly built the Apple QuickTake — one of the first consumer digital cameras — but Apple's logo is on it, not yours. Your engineers ask why Kodak is ghostwriting the future for someone else's brand.

choices.text: Launch a flagship Kodak digital camera line under our own name.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The brand that means 'photography' to every American family now means digital photography too. The transition happens under your logo instead of over your body.

choices.text: Keep white-labeling. Their brand takes the cannibalization risk.
choices.reveal: This is what happened — Kodak built the QuickTake and let Apple wear it. You proved digital cameras were consumer-ready and gave the proof away.

choices.text: Kill the partnership. Stop feeding the digital market entirely.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Canon, Nikon, and Sony don't need your help. The market arrives on schedule — you've just removed yourself from the room where it happens.

decisions.scenario: 1996. Kodak is worth $31B, its all-time peak. Your answer to digital is the Advantix APS system — a $500M bet on a camera that shoots digital previews but still makes customers buy and develop film.

choices.text: Scrap Advantix. Put the $500M into pure digital.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: At peak valuation with $500M to spend, you finally pick a side — the winning one. The war chest exists; this time it buys the future instead of defending the past.

choices.text: Ship Advantix. Digital preview, film purchase. Best of both.
choices.reveal: This is what happened. Kodak spent $500M on a product whose core feature was making you still buy film. It flopped instantly — the 'best of both worlds' was the worst of one.

choices.text: Use the peak: acquire an internet photo-sharing startup.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You buy the company that becomes the place photos live online. (In reality Kodak did buy Ofoto — in 2001, late, and used it to sell prints.) Doing it at the peak, for the right reason, changes the story.

decisions.scenario: 2003. Film sales are finally collapsing. You have one pivot left in the tank. The board is split between printers ('people will always print photos'), a patent-licensing war chest, and a real consumer-digital push.

choices.text: Go all-in on consumer digital cameras and online sharing.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: EasyShare cameras actually briefly led U.S. digital sales — in this timeline you back that win instead of milking it for printer ink. When phones eat cameras, you're the photo platform, not the casket.

choices.text: Pivot to printers and ink. People will always print photos.
choices.reveal: This is what happened. Kodak bet the endgame on inkjet printers just as the world stopped printing photos. Chapter 11 came in January 2012; the patents were sold off for parts.

choices.text: Become a patent troll. Sue everyone using our IP.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The lawsuits fund a slower funeral. Kodak actually did win over $3B in licensing — litigation is a painkiller, not a cure.

bands.line: You invented the future and actually shipped it. Villain episode: WON.
A smaller, humbler Kodak survives selling sensors and printing. Fuji did it — you half did.
You invented the digital camera and died fighting it. Faithful to history. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Sony took the market Kodak invented. Today's agents flagged an incumbent shelving its own disruption →
